retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Teratoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Initial Assessment: It involves an initial assessment of the patient, including vitals, detailed history, physical exam with more emphasis on abdominal and pelvic exam, followed by complete blood count, complete metabolic panel, and a pregnancy test. Mature cystic teratomas do not have any specific diagnostic tumor marker.  Serum tumor markers, in particular, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) have shown to play some role in diagnosis and follow up. Cystic teratomas are usually asymptomatic and often discovered on workup for other conditions. However, in patients presenting with abdominal pain, discomfort, and increasing abdominal girth, imaging plays an important role in further workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lactate Dehydrogenase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with non-seminomatous testicular cancer LDH is used as a staging (S classification) marker.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Cancer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum tumor markers play an important role in the evaluation and management of testicular cancer. Post-orchiectomy markers are part of the tumor staging and risk categorization of testicular cancer and are part of any evaluation of treatment response or of disease relapse.  LDH is found in diverse tissues throughout the body, and LDH-1 is expressed on chromosome 12p, which is over-expressed in germ cell tumors. Higher LDH levels can correspond to higher tumor burden. AFP and β-hCG evaluations are seen in many germ cell tumors. [48]  β-hCG is produced by syncytiotrophoblasts within germ cell tumors and is seen in both non-seminomatous and seminomatous germ cell tumors. Choriocarcinomas commonly express β-hCG, as do approximately 15% of seminomas. AFP derives from the yolk sac, embryonal, and some teratomas within germ cell tumors and is never elevated in pure seminoma or pure choriocarcinoma. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Mediastinal Nonseminoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A complete physical exam including a genital exam is crucial. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (b-HCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) are the tumor markers that can be elevated in these patients [5] . Patients with benign teratomas do not have elevated b-HCG or AFP. Levels of AFP and HCG are elevated in the majority of patients with mediastinal non-seminomas. Cytogenticstudents are often done to rule out Klinefelter syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Nonseminomatous Testicular Tumors -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients with testicular non-seminomatous germ cell tumors should undergo contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis. [50] Patients with high levels of β-hCG should undergo cross-sectional CT imaging of the brain to identify possible metastatic lesions. Post-orchiectomy levels of serum tumor markers are necessary to help check the response to initial treatment and surveillance. Higher LDH levels can suggest a greater tumor burden. AFP and β-hCG evaluations are seen in many germ cell tumors. [54]  β-hCG is produced by the syncytiotrophoblast component within germ cell tumors and is most closely associated with seminomas, although it can present in both seminomatous and non-seminomatous malignancies. β-hCG is commonly expressed by the syncytiotrophoblasts found in choriocarcinoma and approximately 15% of seminomas. Yolk sac tumors, embryonal cell carcinomas, and some teratomas secrete alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Cyst -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When an ovarian mass is suspected, the provider should first determine whether the patient is pre or postmenopausal. If the former is true, the first step is to perform a serum beta hCG or urine pregnancy test. Once pregnancy is ruled out, imaging should be done for further evaluation. A complete blood count should focus on the hematocrit and hemoglobin levels to evaluate for anemia caused by acute bleeding. Urinalysis should be obtained to rule out urinary tract infections and kidney stones. Endocervical swabs should be collected to assess for pelvic inflammatory disease. Cancer antigen 125 (CA125) is a protein present on the cell membrane of healthy ovarian tissues and ovarian carcinomas. A blood level of less than 35U/ml is taken as normal. CA125 values are raised in 85% of patients with epithelial ovarian cancer, while it is raised in 50% of patients with stage I cancer confined to the ovary. The finding of an elevated CA 125 level is most useful when combined with an ultrasound while evaluating a postmenopausal woman with an ovarian cyst. [11] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Ovarian Torsion -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory testing should include a complete blood count, complete metabolic panel, and a serum hCG. CBC may show a leukocytosis, or anemia if the torsion is causing hemorrhage. Hcg is especially important since pregnancy is a risk factor for torsion. These laboratory abnormalities are non-specific, and most often, the lab values will be normal in torsion.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Sex Cord–Stromal Tumors -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation begins with a complete history and physical examination of patients. A careful, focused testicular examination should be performed, along with the assessment for any signs of gynecomastia and abnormal secondary sexual characteristics. Subsequently, routine investigations may be conducted. This includes drawing serum tumor markers and assessing sex hormone levels such as FSH, LH, testosterone, and estradiol. While serum sex hormone levels may be elevated, germ cell tumor markers, including AFP, β-hCG, and LDH, will not be elevated in SCSTs.

retrieved_docs.title: Seminoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with a suspected testicular mass, initial workup includes laboratory tests and imaging results. Serum tumor marker levels, including alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-HCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), are measured. Serum beta-HCG and LDH levels can be elevated in seminomas while AFP is not raised in pure seminomas. LDH is a less specific marker, but levels can correlate with overall tumor burden. Beta-HCG levels are increased in 5-10% of patients with seminoma, and levels may correlate with metastatic disease but not with overall survival. Placenta-like alkaline phosphatase may be elevated in patients with seminoma, especially when the tumor burden increases; however, these may also be increased with smoking.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Cancer -- Staging -- Serum Markers (S)
retrieved_docs.content: SX: marker studies not available or not performed S0: marker study levels within normal limits S1: LDH <1.5 × ULN and HCG <5,000 mIU/ml and AFP <1,000 ng/ml S2: LDH 1.5–10.0 × ULN or HCG 5,000–50,000 mIU/ml or AFP 1,000 to 10,000 ng/ml S3: LDH >10.0 × ULN or HCG >50,000 mIU/ml or AFP >10,000 ng/ml

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Serum laboratory testing should typically include a complete blood count and metabolic profile. [7] The American Society of Clinical Oncology guidelines recommend offering genetic testing for BRCA1 and BRCA2 to all women with epithelial ovarian cancer, and in patients with clear cell, endometrioid, or mucinous ovarian cancer subtypes, MMRd molecular testing should be offered . [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients identified with a testicular tumor should have a full work-up in addition to the ultrasound scan, which includes tumor markers (AFP, LDH, and β-HCG), a baseline serum LH and testosterone, and a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. [178] Testis-sparing surgery may be considered in appropriately selected patients with small neoplasms, negative tumor markers, and no evidence of malignancy on imaging. [1] [2] [7] [29] [126] [127] Contrast-enhanced ultrasonography, color Doppler imaging, elastrography, and MRI should be used preoperatively as necessary to help differentiate benign from malignant testicular neoplasms. [8] [82] [89] [101] [102] [103] [167] [168] [179] [180]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonseminomatous Testicular Tumors -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor Markers : alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), and lactic dehydrogenase (LDH). Serum tumor markers (AFP, HCG, and LDH) should be checked  before any intervention, including orchiectomy, as these may be elevated in non-seminomatous testicular germ cell tumors (NSGCT).

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Cancer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients with testicular germ cell tumors should undergo abdominopelvic imaging with computed tomography (CT). Patients with elevated serum tumor markers (AFP, α-fetoprotein; β-hCG, β-subunit of human chorionic gonadotropin; LDH, lactate dehydrogenase) should be evaluated further with computed tomography (CT) of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis for staging. [39] If testicular tumor markers are within the normal range, the rate of metastasis almost outside of the retroperitoneum is very low; therefore, the addition of a chest CT-scan to cross-sectional imaging of the abdomen and pelvis is highly unlikely to alter the treatment plan, and a chest radiograph suffices when combined with abdominopelvic CT imaging.  Choriocarcinoma has been shown to spread via hematogenous routes, and patients with high levels of β-hCG should undergo cross-sectional imaging of the brain to identify metastatic lesions due to choriocarcinoma hematogenously spreading to the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Indications -- Laboratory Investigations
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory investigations include serum testicular tumor markers, such as AFP, LDH, and β-HCG. [28] [36] [38] [71] Germ cell tumors, including seminomatous and nonseminomatous types, often present with elevated serum tumor markers; however, this finding is not definitive. [28] [36] [37] [38] [71] LH and testosterone levels should be assessed for patients being considered for testis-sparing surgery. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonseminomatous Testicular Tumors -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha-fetoprotein is elevated in 50% to 70% of all patients with NSGCT.  Pure seminomas do not make alpha-fetoprotein, so if it's elevated in a patient diagnosed only with seminoma, it means that NSGCT elements were missed. The patient should be treated as having a mixed germ cell malignancy. Syncytiotrophoblasts manufacture human chorionic gonadotropins in seminomas and a few non-seminomatous germ cell tumors such as choriocarcinoma and embryonal carcinomas. LDH is generally a nonspecific tumor marker that is elevated in 20% to 60% of patients with NSGCT and is generally considered an indicator of bulky disease. Alpha-fetoprotein has a half-life of about 6 days, while the half-life of HCG is 24 to 48 hours.  In practical terms, HCG should begin to return to normal about 1 week after orchiectomy, while alpha-fetoprotein will take a minimum of 5 weeks. AFP and β-hCG are also elevated in various lung, liver, gut, bone, and live tumors. [47] [48] There are new markers to diagnose and evaluate germ cell tumors, like levels of circulating microRNAs, especially miR-371a-3p. However, their precise role in the management of testicular tumors is yet to be determined. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding of cancer of the ovary, indicating the presence of abnormal tissue within the ovarian region. Similarly, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the ovary, representing the malignant cells that drive the disease process. Together, these pathological entities underscore the cellular abnormalities that characterize ovarian cancer and are essential for its diagnosis and understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding of ovarian cancer, indicating the presence of cancerous cells within ovarian tissue. Similarly, Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are described as abnormal cells of ovarian cancer, reflecting cellular atypia that may be difficult to classify but still signifies disease involvement. Together, these cellular abnormalities underscore key diagnostic features of ovarian cancer and are essential for accurate pathology assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2590
retrieved_docs.content: In the context of ovarian cancer, tumor cells that are benign are not considered a disease‑related finding, indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of cancer of ovary. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are classified as abnormal cells of cancer of ovary, reflecting a potential pathological significance that may require further evaluation. This distinction emphasizes the importance of accurately characterizing cellular morphology when assessing ovarian pathology and guiding appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2164
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of ovary is recognized as a distinct malignant disease whose abnormal cellular component is described as “Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant,” indicating that the neoplastic cells in ovarian cancer may initially be difficult to classify but are ultimately considered abnormal cells of the disease. In the diagnostic work‑up, cancer of ovary must be differentiated from سرطان القولون (colon cancer), as both can present with overlapping pelvic or abdominal findings and therefore appear on each other’s differential diagnosis lists. Additionally, cancer of ovary can give rise to or be associated with نقائل إلى المبيض, which denotes metastatic lesions that involve the ovary. Understanding that ovarian cancer may be confused with colon cancer, that its tumor cells are abnormal by nature, and that it can produce ovarian metastases is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells that cannot be definitively classified as benign or malignant are considered abnormal cells in the context of ovarian cancer, indicating that such indeterminate tumor cells represent the pathological cellular component of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding indicative of cancer of ovary, reflecting the presence of malignant cells infiltrating ovarian tissue in patients with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cellular component that defines ovarian cancer, indicating that ovarian cancer is characterized by the presence of these malignant tumor cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2229
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that defines cancer of ovary, indicating that malignant tumor cells constitute the disease entity cancer of ovary. Within the disease framework, cancer of ovary is associated with the concept نقائل إلى المبيض, reflecting that ovarian cancer can give rise to metastases to the ovary. This relationship highlights the pathological link between the malignant cells, the primary ovarian cancer, and its potential spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: The ovary (ovarium) serves as the primary anatomic site for several gynecologic conditions. Disorders that involve both the fallopian tube and the ovary are identified in the ovary, as are inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs that have a finding site in the ovary. Lesions of the ovary are likewise localized to the ovary. In addition to these pathologic processes, the ovary can be the location of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ). Recognizing the ovary as the common finding site for these disorders and as the potential site of ectopic gestation is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: According to the finding_site_of relationship, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are identified with a finding site in the ovarium. In a similar manner, a Lesion of ovary is described as having a has_finding_site in the ovarium. The ovarium itself is linked by a has_finding_site to اضطراب مبيضي (an ovarian disorder) and by a has_location to حمل مَبيضِيّ (an ovarian pregnancy). These interconnected statements highlight that inflammatory conditions, lesions, and specific disorders of the ovary are all centered on the ovary as the primary anatomical site, and they may be associated with rare presentations such as ovarian pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing the ovary’s role in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1901
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding associated with cancer of ovary, indicating that the presence of malignant cells infiltrating tissue is a diagnostic feature of ovarian malignancy. Cancer of ovary also encompasses the concept of نقائل إلى المبيض, which refers to metastatic lesions that spread to the ovary, highlighting that ovarian cancer can involve secondary tumor deposits within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships emphasize that malignant cellular infiltration serves as a key histologic indicator of ovarian cancer and that ovarian cancer may present with metastatic involvement of the ovary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2727
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of fallopian tube and ovary, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesion of ovary, and ovary problem are all medical conditions that have the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the pathological manifestations of each of these entities are localized to ovarian tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingo‑oophorectomy, complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral (separate procedure) is closely related to oophorectomy; unilateral and bilateral, as both surgical interventions involve removal of ovarian tissue and may be performed on one side (unilateral) or both sides (bilateral).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2548
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding indicative of both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). In contrast, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding for either cancer of ovary or السرطان البنكرياسي, indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of these malignancies. Conversely, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as abnormal cells characteristic of both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي, underscoring their role as a key histologic marker of these diseases. This distinction between malignant and benign cellular components helps clinicians interpret biopsy results and guide appropriate patient management for ovarian and pancreatic cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, noninflammatory disorder of ovary, and ovary problem all share the ovarium as their finding site, indicating that these conditions are localized to the ovary. In addition, the ovarium is linked to the location described by the Arabic term “حمل مَبيضِيّ,” which denotes an ovarian pregnancy. Together, these relationships emphasize that both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory ovarian disorders, as well as the specific obstetric condition of ovarian pregnancy, are centered in the ovary, highlighting its central role in the diagnosis and management of these gynecologic and obstetric entities.
